figures.caption: Fig. 1: Comparison between conventional text-space reasoning and Medical Latent Visual Reasoning. In conventional textspace reasoning, the model autoregressively generates intermediate textual reasoning tokens before producing the final answer. In Medical Latent Visual Reasoning, the last hidden states are directly reused as input embeddings to form latent reasoning steps, enabling implicit reasoning in the hidden space before answer generation.

TABLE I: Performance comparison across eight medical imaging modalities. We compare MEDLVR with zero-shot generalpurpose VLMs, zero-shot medical VLMs, and recent reasoning-oriented medical VLMs across eight medical imaging modalities. Our method achieves strong overall performance across heterogeneous medical visual domains. Modality definitions are as follows: CT, computed tomography; MRI, magnetic resonance imaging; X-Ray, X-ray imaging; Ultrasound, ultrasound imaging; Dermoscopy, dermoscopic imaging; Fundus, fundus photography; OCT, optical coherence tomography; and Microscopy, microscopy imaging.

Fig. 3: Visualization of attention allocation and spatial grounding behaviors. Compared with the baseline model, the proposed reasoning process exhibits stronger alignment with clinically relevant regions and a more structured allocation of attention to visual tokens across layers.

Fig. 2: Overview of the proposed MedLVR framework for medical VQA. The top panel shows the autoregressive multimodal reasoning pipeline, in which visual, textual, latent, and special embeddings are jointly processed by the multimodal language model. Rather than generating long textual reasoning chains, MedLVR reuses hidden states between <Medlvr Start> and <Medlvr End> as latent reasoning steps before producing the final answer. The bottom panel illustrates the two-stage training strategy: Stage 1 uses ROI-supervised latent alignment to regularize intermediate latent states, while Stage 2 applies reinforcement learning with the VLPO objective to directly optimize latent reasoning and answer generation.

TABLE II: Performance comparison on medical VQA benchmarks. We compare MedLVR with proprietary frontier MLLMs, open-source general-purpose VLMs, medical MLLMs, multimodal medical agents, and recent reasoning-oriented multimodal models across five out-of-domain medical VQA benchmarks: SLAKE, VQA-RAD, PMC-VQA, MMMU (Health & Medicine), and MedXpertQA. Bold and underlined numbers denote the best and second-best results in each column, respectively. The superscript values in the MedLVR row indicate the absolute improvement over the base Qwen2.5-VL-7B model. Avg. denotes the arithmetic mean over all available benchmark scores in each row, while entries marked as “–” indicate that the corresponding method is not evaluated or not reported on that benchmark and are therefore excluded from the average calculation.
